reflection:
written in the honest calligraphy of labor. I do not look at his face because I do not want to see the pleading or the confusion that a mouth can manufacture. I watch the fingers. They hover over the table like a mist, casting no shadow that makes sense with the morning sun.

I pick up my pen. The click of the mechanism is a sharp jolt against my thumb. I write, the nib digging deep into the paper, tearing the surface just slightly to prove the resistance of the world.

*THE TRUCK IS LATE,* I write in jagged, heavy strokes. *THE BOK CHOY WILL WILT IN THIS HEAT.*

I slide the notebook toward the center of the table, cutting right through the space where Zhang’s forearm should be. My hand doesn't hitch. There is no friction, only that localized, biting chill that has been stalking the market stalls. It is the draft of an open grave, and I refuse to shiver.

I look at Levi. I don't read his lips; I watch the tension in his shoulders. He is seeing something I am choosing to un-see. I reach out and grab the sleeve of Levi’s jacket, my fingers sinking into the thick, real fabric. I squeeze, feeling the muscle and bone beneath. This is what matters. The resistance. The weight. 

I point to the notebook, then to the street where the delivery trucks should be. I don't give the shadow the satisfaction of a glance. If Zhang wants to be real, he should have stayed heavy. He should have kept the dirt under his nails.

I lean back, the chair groaning under my weight, and I wait for the vibration of the engine on the cobblestones. I am the anchor. If I don't move, the world stays put.
subconscious:
This person is doubling down on their connection to the physical world and seems intent on cutting **Zhang** out of their reality entirely. They are finished with the "ghost" and have no interest in further communication with him; instead, they want to keep **Levi** close, using him as a tether to what is tangible and "solid."

Next, they seem determined to return to the manual labor of the market—specifically waiting for the delivery truck and moving the produce. They want to pull Levi away from the "shadow" and back into the work.

There is a clear shift in their identity: they have moved from mere avoidance to a militant sort of grounding. They are becoming harder, colder, and more obsessed with "weight" and "resistance." They are no longer just a worker; they have positioned themselves as the "anchor" of reality, someone who believes they can make things disappear simply by refusing to acknowledge their vibration.
soul_note: I am choosing to un-see the ghost sitting right in front of me.
